name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَنتُمْ سُكَـٰرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ تَعْلَمُوا۟ مَا تَقُولُونَ وَلَا جُنُبًا إِلَّا عَابِرِى سَبِيلٍ حَتَّىٰ تَغْتَسِلُوا۟ ۚ وَإِن كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ أَوْ جَآءَ أَحَدٌ مِّنكُم مِّنَ ٱلْغَآئِطِ أَوْ لَـٰمَسْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا۟ مَآءً فَتَيَمَّمُوا۟ صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا فَٱمْسَحُوا۟ بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا (٤٣)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not approach prayer when you are intoxicated, [not] until you know what you are saying, nor [enter mosques] in the state of ritual impurity until you have washed yourselves, except while passing through. But if you are sick or on a journey, or any of you has come from the toilet, or you have touched women, and you cannot find water, then make your ablution on clean ground and wipe a part of your faces and your hands. Indeed Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving. (43)
```

## (Commentary)

It has already been mentioned in the discussion regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ** (They ask you concerning wine and gambling) [al-Baqarah: 219], that the verses addressing the matter of wine consist of five groups. Combining these verses with one another indicates that this verse: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَقْرَبُوا** (O you who have faith! Do not approach...) was revealed after His saying, the Exalted: **تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَراً وَرِزْقاً حَسَناً** (you derive from it an intoxicant and a good provision) [al-Nahl: 67], and His saying: **قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ** (Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies, the outward among them and the inward, and sin...’) [al-A‘raf: 33]. It was revealed before His saying, the Exalted: **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهما أَكْبَرُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِما** (They ask you concerning wine and gambling. Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them, and some profits for the people, but their sinfulness is greater than their profit’) [al-Baqarah: 219], and His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنْصابُ وَالْأَزْلامُ رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ فَاجْتَنِبُوهُ** (O you who have faith! Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and the divining arrows are abominations of Satan’s doing, so avoid them) [al-Ma’idah: 90], the latter being the last of the verses to be revealed.

It is possible, in one respect, to conceive of the chronological order differently from what we have mentioned, such that the first to be revealed would be the verse of *al-Nahl*, then *al-A‘raf*, then *al-Baqarah*, then *al-Nisa’*, and finally *al-Ma’idah*. The implication of this order regarding the story of the definitive prohibition of drinking wine would differ from the previous order. In this case, what is in *Surah al-A‘raf* would be a prohibition without further explanation; then what is in *Surah al-Baqarah* would be a decisive prohibition, yet the Muslims continued to find excuses to avoid total abstention until they were strictly forbidden from it during the state of prayer (الصلاة) in *Surah al-Nisa’*; and finally, an absolute prohibition in all circumstances was revealed in *Surah al-Ma’idah*. However, if you reflect upon the contents of the verses, you would likely prefer the previous order over this one. It is improbable that after the explicit prohibition found in the verse of *al-Baqarah*, a prohibition restricted only to the state of prayer—as found in the verse of *al-Nisa’*—would be revealed. Thus, this verse must precede the verse of *al-Baqarah*, unless we say that the prohibition of prayer while intoxicated is an allusion (كناية) to praying while lethargic (كسلان), as mentioned in some of the forthcoming narrations.

As for the placement of the verse between those preceding and following it, it appears as an intercalated or parenthetical (المعترضة) statement. However, there is a possibility that might justify this type of intercalation—which is not uncommon in the Qur’an—namely, that a number of verses with a single, continuous, and harmonious context (سياق) may be revealed gradually over several days. During this time, a need arises for the revelation of a verse or verses before the original sequence is completed. Consequently, the verse falls between the other verses like a parenthetical insertion. In reality, it is not foreign (أجنبية) to the context; rather, it is like speech within speech intended to dispel a misconception that must be addressed or to fulfill a pressing need for its mention. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted:

**بَلِ الْإِنْسانُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ وَلَوْ أَلْقى مَعاذِيرَهُ لا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا جَمْعَهُ وَقُرْآنَهُ فَإِذا قَرَأْناهُ فَاتَّبِعْ قُرْآنَهُ ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا بَيانَهُ كَلَّا بَلْ تُحِبُّونَ الْعاجِلَةَ** (Rather man is a witness against himself, though he should offer his excuses. Do not move your tongue with it to hasten it. Indeed, its collection and its recitation are Our charge. So when We have recited it, follow its recitation. Then its exposition is also Our charge. No indeed! Rather you love the fleeting life) [al-Qiyamah: 14–20]. Observe the placement of the segment from His saying: **لا تُحَرِّكْ** (Do not move...) to His saying: **بَيانَهُ** (its exposition).

Based on this, there is no need for strained efforts (التكلف) to explain the link between the verse and what precedes it, or the link between what follows it and the verse itself. Furthermore, the Qur’an was revealed in stages (نجوما), and such a link is only required in *Surahs* revealed all at once or in verses with an obvious continuity that reveals the connection between them.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (O you who have faith!) until His saying: **ما تَقُولُونَ** (what you say): What is meant by prayer (الصلاة) here is the mosque (المسجد). The evidence for this is His saying: **وَلا جُنُباً إِلَّا عابِرِي سَبِيلٍ** (nor while you are in a state of ritual impurity (جنباً)—except when passing through). The reason for this metaphorical usage (التجوز) is His saying: **حَتَّى تَعْلَمُوا ما تَقُولُونَ** (until you know what you say). For if it were said, "Do not approach the mosque while you are intoxicated," the justification "until you know what you say" would not be sound, or the justification would imply a meaning other than what was intended. The intended meaning is to convey that while in prayer, you are facing the Station of Grandeur and Majesty and addressing the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين); thus, it is not fitting for you to be intoxicated and to nullify your intellects with the abomination (رجس) of wine such that you do not know what you are saying. This meaning—as you can see—suits the prohibition of approaching the prayer. However, since prayer most often occurs in the mosque in congregation—according to the *Sunnah*—and since the intent was also to mention the rulings for one in a state of ritual impurity (الجنب) regarding entering the mosque, the discourse was condensed and the speech was molded in the manner you see.

Accordingly, His saying: **حَتَّى تَعْلَمُوا ما تَقُولُونَ** (until you know what you say) serves as the justification (التعليل) for the prohibition of drinking wine such that its intoxication remains until the time of entering the prayer. That is: "We have forbidden you from it so that you may know what you say." It is not a "limit" (غاية) for the ruling in the sense of "do not approach until you know what you say, and once you know what you say, there is no harm."

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا جُنُباً إِلَّا عابِرِي سَبِيلٍ** (nor while you are in a state of ritual impurity—except when passing through) to the end of the verse: The discussion regarding this verse will follow in the commentary (تفسير) of His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا قُمْتُمْ إِلَى الصَّلاةِ** (O you who have faith! When you stand up for prayer...) [al-Ma’idah: 6].

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Muhammad ibn al-Fadl, from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: **لا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلاةَ وَأَنْتُمْ سُكارى حَتَّى تَعْلَمُوا ما تَقُولُونَ** (Do not approach prayer when you are intoxicated, until you know what you say). He said: "This was before wine was forbidden."

I say: This narration should be understood to mean that the "prohibition of wine" refers to the clarification of its prohibition. Otherwise, it would contradict the Book, for the verse of *al-A‘raf* explicitly forbids wine under the heading of "sin" (إثم), and the verse of *al-Baqarah* declares that in wine there is "great sin." Thus, wine was forbidden in Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة), as *Surah al-A‘raf* is Meccan, and no one disputes that this verse (the verse of *al-Nisa’*) is Medinan. There are several narrations similar to this one from Sunni sources stating that the verse was revealed before the prohibition of wine. It is also possible that the narration considers the meaning of the verse to be about praying while lethargic (كسلان).

In the same source, from Zurarah, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "Do not stand for prayer lethargically (متكاسلاً), nor drowsily (متناعساً), nor heavily (متثاقلاً), for these are among the flaws of hypocrisy (النفاق). Indeed, Allah forbade the believers from standing for prayer while they are intoxicated (سكارى)—meaning from sleep."

I say: His saying, "for these are among the flaws of hypocrisy," is an inference drawn by him (peace be upon him) from His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (O you who have faith!). Thus, one who rebels against this address is a hypocrite, not a believer. His saying, "meaning from sleep," could be the words of the narrator or the words of the Imam (peace be upon him), serving as an interpretation of the verse from the perspective of the inner meaning (بطن) of the Qur’an, or it could be from its outer meaning (الظهر).

Other narrations have been reported interpreting it as sleep, narrated by al-Ayyashi in his *Tafsir* from al-Halabi in two narrations, and by al-Kulayni in *al-Kafi* with his chain to Zayd al-Shahham from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), and with his chain to Zurarah from al-Baqir (peace be upon him). This meaning was also narrated by al-Bukhari in his *Sahih* from Anas from the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny).
